reviews.review_text:
I'm not far away from giving this game a positive review. I've certainly played enough hours.  
The foundations of the game's ships are some of the best that space sims have to offer. The ships feel and sound incredible.

But, as common as the sentiment might be, I feel it's important to talk about the game's depth, because I don't really see this particular perspective discussed enough.  
At a certain point, the basic novelty wears off and you see the underlying system more clearly. 
Pretty much everything loops back into the same mission board cycle and monetary/rank based ship progression.  
Unlocking/fitting out later ships of course allows you to tackle more difficult missions and targets, but... That's really it. Nothing else really changes alongside you.  

The depth, here, isn't so much about a lack of content. It's about a lack of meaningful context. What's missing is a sense of purpose, of belonging, of consequence, recognition.  

The factions are the best example I have of this.  
You can  join the Federation or Empire and work your way up their ranks - but nothing meaningful about the game changes at any point. 
You can unlock faction-specific ships, but your relationship to the faction feels fundamentally the same as not being associated to that faction at all. It's the same recycled missions as before, just with a faction symbol tied to it, and nothing of real consequence or progress other than a number increasing over time. 

I want joining a faction to actually feel like joining a faction. I want to defend their territory, support their logistics, participate in offensives, respond to strategic threats and feel like I occupy a real role within a faction or military, like my rank means something and carries duty, responsibility. 
Not necessarily through scripted linear content; there's no need to scrap the mission board foundation. But what I feel is missing is systems that make the factions and wider systems react to who you are and how you're committing yourself. 
  
And honestly, I don't think Elite is that far away from having a basic, achievable version of that kind of immersion that could be expanded on over time.
 
I've heard people respond these kinds of criticims by saying "It's a sandbox" or something to the effect of "You make your own goals" but I find this to be dismissive, as it neglects to see how we can have both.
The game already has an excellent foundation. What's missing, for me, is the substance inside the sandwich.

[b]6.5k hours later I guess I can finally write a review.[/b]

It all started somewhere around autumn 2020 when Elite Dangerous was free on Epic Games. I picked it up with zero expectations. Just another free game to try, maybe spend a few evenings on and forget about. Instead, I’ve been stuck here ever since.

At the beginning I had no idea what I was doing. Docking felt like a boss fight, every jump felt risky, and making a few thousand credits actually felt like progress. It was slow, confusing, and honestly kind of clunky. But something about it kept pulling me back in. Not in an addictive “one more match” way, but more like a quiet curiosity. I wanted to understand it.

And that’s how it starts with Elite Dangerous. You don’t get hooked instantly. It grows on you.

You learn how to fly. Then how to survive. Then how to optimize. At some point you stop thinking about controls and start thinking in routes, systems, distances. The galaxy stops being overwhelming and starts becoming… familiar.

From there it just escalates.

I’ve done pretty much everything this game has to offer. Trading, exploration, combat, exobiology, engineering, long-haul travel. I didn’t specialize early on, I just kept going deeper into whatever felt interesting at the time. Eventually that turns into something else entirely. You stop playing “activities” and start setting your own goals. Fought global thargoid war, killed titan thargoids, made the absurd amount of money from that and i want more. 

That’s where most of my time went.

Long-term projects. The kind that sound cool at the start and then slowly turn into a grind. You tell yourself it’s worth it. Halfway through you’re not so sure anymore. But you keep going anyway because stopping feels worse than finishing.

I had moments where I was completely burned out. Logging in felt more like obligation than fun. The game has a way of doing that if you let it. It doesn’t always respect your time, and it definitely doesn’t try to keep you entertained every second.

And yet I kept coming back.

Part of it is just how the game feels. Flying a ship in Elite still hits different even after thousands of hours. The sound design, the scale, the way space actually feels empty instead of artificially busy. You can spend hours doing almost nothing and somehow it still works. There’s a kind of atmosphere here that other games try to replicate but never quite get right.

But it’s not all good.

Odyssey release was rough. Probably the roughest period this game has ever had. Performance issues, bugs, design choices that made you question what they were even going for. It felt unfinished, and for a lot of people that was the breaking point.

I didn’t quit, but I won’t pretend it didn’t affect the experience. That was one of those moments where you really see both sides of this game. The potential and the frustration at the same time.

Then there was the Thargoid war.

That’s one of the things Elite actually did right. A large-scale conflict that players could take part in. Not just background lore, but something you could actively engage with. I fought in it, took part in Titan kills, watched systems fall and get pushed back. It wasn’t perfect, but it felt like being part of something bigger than just your own progress.

Moments like that remind you why you stuck around.

Somewhere along the way I also started doing stuff outside the game. Made a video ranking all ships with memes, because after this many hours you either go insane or start creating things out of it.

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-VDRUTXFwf8

And at some point I realized I actually have a full commander history written down:

https://inara.cz/elite/cmdr-biography/319802/

Looking at that now is kind of surreal. You don’t notice the time passing while you’re playing. It just accumulates. Then suddenly you’re sitting on thousands of hours and a whole timeline of things you’ve done.

So yeah, is Elite Dangerous a good game?

That depends on what you expect.

If you want something fast, rewarding, constantly exciting, this probably isn’t it. The game is slow. It’s repetitive. It will waste your time if you’re not careful. There are systems that feel outdated and mechanics that could have been improved years ago.

But if you’re the kind of person who enjoys long-term progression, setting your own goals, and just existing in a massive world without being rushed… then it’s something special.

This isn’t a game you “finish”.

It’s a game you stay in for a while.

Sometimes you leave for weeks or months. Then you come back, do a few jumps, hear the engines again, and it just clicks like it always did.

I started this thinking I’d play for a few hours because it was free.

6.5k hours later, I’m writing a review on a different platform after transferring my account, still coming back to it from time to time.

That probably says more than anything else I could write here.

[b]Recommended.[/b]

But only if you understand what you’re signing up for.

1000 Hours of Elite have taught me one thing. Even a game as wide as an ocean and as deep as a puddle, can be fun. 

But only if you like to grind. 

The cons:


The controls: Open the setting and just take a few minutes to look through it all. Then suffer.

Starting Elite for the first time, is like being thrown from a bridge. If the thought of having our galaxy in a game, in a decently realistic way, doesn't blow your mind, having to setup your keybinds and controls will. God save your soul if you use a hotas. Thousand hours in and I'm still finding new things to bind but I'm slowly running out of space on my keyboard. 

The grind: This is something that will decide if you stick with Elite or leave it. There's 3 types of grind in elite, 4 if you want to split on foot and your ships.

The credit grind is, well, laughable these days. It's not 2016 anymore, where getting a fully outfitted, A rated Federal corvette or Anaconda was a big deal. You can get a few million credits in a reasonable amount of time, throw together a mandalay and jump out. Do exo biology for a week, then return with enough credits to fund either your fleet for the next 2 years or buy a fleet carrier and fully equip it. 

*The superpower rank grind: Don't.* 

Listen, the Federal Corvette is amazing, the Imperial Cutter is not my kind of ship. There's a but, a big but. This grind is either long and tedious, doing it through combat in sol or other federal systems. Medium long, if you torture yourself through data delivery missions. Or you can do it decently fast with a Panther Clipper by doing "Mining missions", where you just buy said mined resources from stations. 

Why do I say don't? Well, it's a pain. A massive one. I did my Corvette grind in a Type 9, which has less cargo space than the Panther Clipper Mk2 and it took me a week of grinding. I have to say, the mission board didn't treat me nicely, so experiences will vary. But there's a better way. 

Doing it organically. Take missions here and there, make sure it's a minor faction associated with the federation and finish them. After that, if you want to do more, do it. Then you take a break. Elite isn't a game where you're meant to reach the highest tier of equipment in a week or two but this is something I'll get to later on. 

The engineering grind: Get started early. 

The engineering grind is the most important part for both your ship and your on foot gear. 
It is what allows you to get stronger, once you have an a rated ship. Nearly every module can be engineered, weapons for example, can be engineered for more power efficiency, longer range or more damage. Armour can be engineered to be lightweight or give more resistances and armor. 

Your on foot suits and hand held weapons do work differently. At the pioneer supplies shop, you can rank them up to level 5. Then you can add on things like a scope or reload speed in the case of weapons and things like faster movement speed or night vision in the case of suits. 

The other difference between the ship engineering and on foot gear is the materials that you use. The materials for ships, are gathered only by you. Raw, data and manufactured materials for your ship, cannot be traded or bought from other players, though they can be traded at a material trader on certain stations. The materials for on foot gear, can be bought at the bar keeper on player carriers, if they have it enabled and stocked up. Or you can gather them yourself by raiding settlements. 

Those are the 3 (4 if you count ship and on foot engineering seperately due to their differences) true grinds in elite. 

There is also the Pilots federation ranks, though these are gathered organically, by playing the game and doing it's different activities. These ranks, for example combat, only give a decal with each rank. Though getting at least 1 Elite rank in any of the 5 categories, will give you access to Shinrarta Dezhra. In this system, there's the station Jamesons Memorial, which sells every module, ship, on foot weapon and suit for a discount. 

These are my notable cons.

Next is the middle ground, things that are for me, stuck between being a con and a pro.

The galaxy

The galaxy in Elite, is realistc, for the most part at least. There is things, like certain star types, most notably neutron stars and white dwarf stars being all a single types, instead of for example in the case of neutron stars, there being it's different types, magnetar, pulsar, etc. 

But, you have to be aware of one thing.
Like space in real life, for 99,99% of it, once you've seen it once, you've seen it all. 

Do not expect to be able to land on earth like planets. This isn't no mans sky. It's only planets without a atmosphere or planets with a thin atmosphere. So no weird animals to see. Just things that can barely be considered alive.

The game is still unbelievably beautiful and a pretty sight is most often less than two jumps away.
Which is where we get into our next topic. 

The gameplay loops. These are my personal opinion, everybody will differ here, since everybody will like a different thing for a different reason.

Whilst we're on the topic of exploration, let's talk about the gameplay loop of it. You jump into a system and use the D scanner, giving you a number of bodies in the system. From there, you use the full spectrum system scanner, look at the bodies, unconvering them on the system map. Then you can send probes to them via the detailed surface scanner. 

That's it for space exploration and where we get into the other side of explo, exo biology. 
Once you've scanned a planet with the detailed surface scanner, you can see what kind of exo life is on it, if there is any. 

The exo life, I'm talking about here, is bacterium and such things, maybe a little grass. Scan it, move a few hundred meters away, scan it, move another few hundred meters and then you've got it's data which you can sell, just like with the scans you do with your ship.

Though, just like when you're the first to find a planet, you get the first found bonus, meaning extra money. This is especially lucrative with exo biology, with for example the Stratum Tectonitas exo life, which, with the first found bonus, is worth around 90 million. 

That's the exploration gameplay loop. 

Trading is next and I won't say much here. It's lucrative, it's boring. You ship stuff from place a to b, or ship passengers a to b. That's it. Mining techincally falls under this, since you sell your mined goods. But Trading is the easiest way to make money early and the easiest to get to Elite as a rank, giving you easy access to Shinrarta Dezhra. 

Now come the pro's, including the last gameplay loop.
 
Sound design is absolutely beautiful. The ship sounds, the weapon sounds, everything is beautifully crafted and arguably the strongest design point in Elite. I won't say much, it's best to experience it for yourself. 

Ship design, in my opinion, is well done. There's of course ships I don't like design wise, like the Imperial Cutter. But that is personal taste. From a raw stand point, taking only design into account, all ships are done very well. 

The story of Elite Dangerous, is well crafted. Though you, dear CMDR, are not the protagonist.  We are just like in real life, people living in this world. All with our own story, getting to see the bigger story. 

Now the last gameplay loop, combat. 
I'll start with the on foot combat, since I like it less than ship combat. 
It has it's problems, no doubt. Especially with the weapons. FDEV recently put out a few changes to them, making it better. But it is by no doubt perfect. The weapons feel decent, especially the Plasma Sniper rifle and side arm. 
NPCS aren't completely brain dead anymore either, still pretty easy to beat all around if you know what you're doing and can aim. 
Now, ship combat. You don't do it for the money, you do it for the feeling. That's all i'll say. It's good, very well done. 

Experience it yourself

I originally bought this game on console. I met one of my closest friends through this game. The community in this game is a fun mix. 

That aside, the flight model is very good and the variety of ships is also on the good side. A 1:1 scale of the milky way galaxy? Sounds insane on paper but in execution it turns out that equates to vast amounts of nothing. 

This game is for people who have a deep passion for space, space exploration, space capitalism, space piracy, space bounty hunting, space autism, space alcohol, space trees, space guns, space milk, space potato, space space, and space. 

Although Odyssey is way more stable now, the NPC behaviour is on par with Oblivion which is something I suppose.

The scale of the galaxy blows my mind every time I log in. 400 Billion Star Systems. Had to fact check myself that it was billion with a 'B'.

I've done asteroid mining, cargo hauls, and now exploration and this is quickly becoming one of my favorite games I've ever picked up. Be warned, it's an incredibly rough game as a new player and I recommend as many guides as you can get your hands on, but the payoff when you finally get into the flow-state is so good.

Take breaks from it often, it can and will burn you out, as it is a repetitive game at times. But, take in the scenery, enjoy the galaxy and get curious about it. You see a nebula? Make a plan and plot a course to it! They are beautiful!!!

No, you cannot walk around in your ship and no, you can't walk on earth-like worlds. But the other features of the game, to me, really make the game special. I recommend it to any fan of space stuff who is willing to learn.

this game rocks if you like flying in space, especially since the controls feel awesome, but its very repetitive. To give you an example, without any research on how to make money, I decided to grind for a good ship, the alliance chieftain which was somewhere in the ball park of 46 million credits. This took my roughly a week straight of grinding bounties during covid, when I had the time. Now that I don't? forget about it. I still love the game, just keep it in mind, if you don't enjoy a grindy game, this might not be the game for you.

Its asking me for a 25 digit code i dont have. after i just downloaded and signed in to there account. I have no idea whyI cant play the game. anyway. thats why i am asking for a refund and giving you a bad review.

Elite Meh

I did basically everything possible and was 100% bored the entire time. The content is clear as mud and full of bugs. Every time you even come close to enjoying the game there will be 'gameplay' inserted which is nothing but false complexity forcing you to fiddle with systems designed to waste your time.

I made first footfall and got my name on the galaxy map with any ship I want and over 1 billion in the bank..... whoopie do. I am going to do anything else now.
downloaded game was met with you need a frontier account made an account was told i don't own the game refunded game

Great visuals, but every system is a pain to deal with. Nothing is explained, and sources of information are outdated by several years. Another old game where only the people that always played it have space in the community. New players are ostracized.

I can't get the game to launch with controllers.  On every launch attempt, I get the error "Can't connect to Frontier servers".  I've tried many "fixes" I found online but with no results.  I don't understand why a game can't just work.  I certainly won't consider any other Frontier games in the future.

And now, poised with a couple of tricks to try, their server wants a 25 digit code before it will allow me to login to their site.  25 pin code... I read they ditched that scheme and replaced with a Steam account link.

And try to find their site... no, not the storefront, and no... not the support... the LOGIN PAGE... I can't find it to login.

  To say that I am angry I bought this substandard software would truly be an understatement!  No more Frontier games.  I wasted enough money.

The game is outdated, the graphics are pathetic. I have played for many hours, but unfortunately, boredom grows with every hour of playing. This galaxy is empty. Repeatedly repeating the same Gameloops. It's a waste of time. The customer service is very poor; you don't receive any feedback if something doesn't work.

Probably the best sci-fi space game I own, minus the community.  I don't want to get forced into solo or group play just to be a space trucker, I want to ride the astral highway with my fellow truckers!  Trying to truck anything in the bubble via open play is like trying to sneak an ounce of crude through the strait of hormuz, it's incredible and I hate it.

You have to sign up for Frontier account to play the game, so I sign up and....NOTHING.  No message, no active account, no email for verification, just a newly purchased game that will not move past the initial account signup screen.  I've got no use for a company that cannot build and/or maintain usable UI for its customers.

Interesting and fun. Having come from No Mans Sky, this game certainly scratches the "simulation" aspect hello games is missing. Prepare to grind much more comparatively. I highly recommend a HOTAS flight controller.

A close contender to best game ever with Minecraft. 
No linear path and a plethora of gameplay style to choose from. 
This open sandbox let's you live in space and do what you want, when you want. At time it can feel grindy but so does life.

They are building this game the way Star Citizen should have. Started with one concept and now they are adding more and more into it.

Fly the galaxy and with the DLC disembark on the planet surfaces. Its an in between of Star Citizen (which is less arcadey, persistant universe) and No Man's Sky (more arcadey, not mmo persistant universe). Elite Dangerous can be both solo or mmo but still living within the same galaxy as everyone else.

You aren't going to be able to get out of your ship in space or do any kind of pvp boarding or anything like that but it almost has everything Star Citizen has just in a more confined way.

I had no idea what it was until I saw it on sale though. I didn't even know a game like this existed outside of Star Citizen. I'm glad I got it because after painfully play testing Star Citizen, I found this polished shiny gem. 

I'm having a good time just casually cruising the galaxy, hauling, exploring unknown sections of space, and eventually when I'm rich enough I'll do combat and after that colonization (if I make it that far)

With the DLC - You can disembark on planets and check out the stations and bases, or just explore planets for research purposes.

With all that said. I'll beat the dead horse. I want to be able to walk around in my ship. There I gave in and said it.

Quite possibly the best sci-fi space-sim game I've ever played. Took me 800 hours to learn all the systems, how engineering works, how deep-space exploration works and finally got a carrier. 10/10 would recommend and have. Not the biggest fan of SCO as personally I feel it cheapens the experience a bit.

This game has a very incredibly complex user interface with the same key binding doing different things depending on what mode you are in.  It's difficult to keep track of what is doing what.  It's very unforgiving in that way.  

It can be an incredible grind, and from a game design point of view, it doesn't make that much sense.  Apparently (since I gave up on the game at this point), you need to custom engineer your components to get optimum/maximum performance from your ships systems.  To do so, you have to meet certain experience requirements to get the attention of an engineer, then you need to have some key items to give that engineer, then you have to have or go acquire key components to do the upgrades.  There being multiple different upgrades, each level having different component requirements....but... you can only "pin" 1 blue print per engineer...and that goes for all the main systems and weapon and sheild systems on your ship.  Plan to spend some significant time working your way through all that, then either spending actual money to buy a decent ship, or spending more time grinding to get the ship you want for the kind of gameplay you want to do, and I don't know, between 500-1000 hours and I guess you get to start actually having fun with the game.

Too much time, too much grind, too complex of a GUI, and too much 'cost' if you make a mistake.  IF I had unlimited time, I'd probably stick it out as I do see the allure of this game, but, nope, I have real job and a part time business that I run.  I'm looking for something that I don't need to spend so many hours on to even get to the point where it's more fun and less grind.  

So, if you're like me with limited gaming time, then I would recommend finding something else.

Terrible controlls. terrible user interface. the UI is nowhere to be found. it doesnt explain squat it just throws you inside the space and boom. search on google or AI platforms of "how to" and "where to".its just another terrible game without any explaination

Lost in space — and not in a good way

Visually stunning galaxy, but the onboarding is practically nonexistent. The game hands you a spaceship, points vaguely at the universe, and expects you to figure everything out on your own. Fine system is a perfect example — you'll rack up fines without any clear explanation of what you did wrong, and good luck finding out how to pay them. For a game with this much depth, the tutorial barely scratches the surface. No wonder the playerbase is small; the learning cliff sends most newcomers packing before it gets good.
Gankers and pvpers ruin the game for new players. Get rid of them. Find a way. We don't want them anymore.

The mechanics are pretty cool. Lot's of variety, plenty of missions and factions. Endless pros.
Cons:
-time waster: it will EAT your time, takes ages to get places and those places look relatively.. the same
-stupidly expensive token-esque game currency system: I can earn 400 ox or whatever a week to buy the on sale digital ship which has no value outside the game and not a lot inside it, since I can by it with regular in-game currency earned in a matter of hours. OR, I can buy minimum 38 Americano doll hairs worth of ox-♥♥♥♥ currency to buy the eye candy today, which, by the way, is double what I paid for the game.
-addictive: said unreasonable and absurd choice to buy ox to get the shipy-ship worth double the deluxe version of the game starts to make my ding-dong go bing-bong until I once again actually LOOK at the necessary clams to shell out. 
-lots of unspoken mechanics and trial by fire... unless you like.. ick reading, because it will take research levels of it to figure out why I keep F#@king up and missing this station by zipping past it at blisteringly fast bug-splattered-on-a-planetoid-windshield speeds
Other than that A+, room for improvement
Waste of time and money, spent all my time grinding for money just to lose it all due to a single ship

It is worth picking up on sale if you enjoy space sims. The game is grindy and empty. Being a 1 to 1 scale of the Milky Way is crazy cool. It is also fairly bare bones for a space sim. Quite a lot of quality of life that has been in sims even as far back as the 80's is not in this game. For example a match target speed button. That is a dev choice for they wish combat to be more skill based. It still takes skill with a match target speed. It does not have to stay matched but to not have a match speed at the time the button was pressed is just crazy. Quite a lot of the HUD for combat is less than current technology of today. In a space sim. Or water or trees on any planets. (I have not been to Earth yet so perhaps there is on Earth?) Or the ability to properly vector your planet landings with instruments. That is crazy. Nor the ability to mark a location on a planet so you can come back to that spot in the future. That would be much needed with how the bio data collecting works. It is extremely barebones for a space sim made when it was made. Ten years is still modern for a space sim. Every space sim out has more to it other than the one to one Milky Way. (Again that is very cool.)

i can't say its good but that only because im nit picking on a few issues. it seems to open up a bit more after you grind through more of it but with how tedious the traveling is and how your forced to go around the whole system instead of going the direct path idk i cant recommend it

Star Citizen if it wasn't a cash grab and had actual gameplay. Never growing tired of Elite, the game always has new content and many different types of gameplay to enjoy, so if fighting isn't your strength you can always have fun trading, mining or whatever. 
And we've been treated with great content in the past years, the Thargoid war, bunch of excellent new ships, etc. An all-in-one chill gaming gem. 

Fly dangerous, cmdrs! o7

Nothing but a overpriced "Waiting Simulator". Want to fly to a planet..?? Then wait 5 mins to get to its system, then slow down and wait another 5 minutes.. then slow down even more and spend another 5 mins to land. Then do your mission that takes 1 minute... and then do the same thing to go back. wait and repeat.
I read endless excuses there's a hard  "learning curve"... What they mean is the game has a trash system that's all mashed together and makes no sense. more like ideas they didn't know how to implement, so it just got tossed in. Ground combat is garbage, another after thought added as filler. 3rd time i tried to get into this game, wont be a 4th.

The first time I played this game, I was extremely confused. A few confusing google searches later and I got through the first part. Then, I load up the game thinking I kind of know what im doing, but soon figure out I dont. Finally actually start a mission, take 20 minutes trying to figure out where the planet I have to go to is, another 20 minutes getting to the spot where I have to deliver some package, get denied access, wait around, get accepted then proceed to try finding a building that has the information of the package and get shot up. Additionally, I tried to enter my ship, but the loading screen took so long that by time I entered, my ship had already been blown up.

I just couldn't get into it, and I like space sims. I even had more fun playing Avorion than this. Shoot, Interstellar Rift was also a better experience. It doesn't have the character of a beloved entry into the genre such as the "X" series, and without the building aspects of a game like Avorion or Interstellar Rift, I think you're better served by getting into Star Citizen, which is similar enough, and excels at everything this game tries to double down on to set it apart from other titles in the genre. I could only recommend this if you are very specifically playing it for a good HOTAS experience on a budget, which this game can offer when it is on sale. However, if you're looking for a good space sim that has more to offer than that, I'd recommend other entries in the genre like X4. Also, if you are really wanting the best HOTAS experience, you really should opt for Star Citizen instead. For me this was a 4/10.

I was quite enjoying the experience, bought the game, put on my VR headset, did the tutorial and (eventually once I'd found that I had to go back to the main menu) the training missions. I then finished the starter zone stuff, did a few courier missions, then the "go explore the galaxy" mission to make me leave the newbie zone. I upgraded my ship and started doing some trading, did a few runs, then found I was super bored. The game really doesn't tell you what else is available, it feels like - is this it? it's kinda boring. 

Yet every youtube guide I look at they all say "this game is amazing and so much fun!" Where is the fun? What goals can I set for myself? Right now it feels like the trading is just going back and forth not seeing anything interesting, combat is too dangerous, and the first time I tried to go exploring I ran out of fuel and respawned back at the start. Also I'm in VR so have no interest in the ground based stuff because I heard it just pastes a 2D image over your headset display (seriously?! I thought this game was designed from ground-up with VR focus). It also sucks that in order to use VR controls you need to download a mod which is a little janky. AND if you want to be able to make any money at all in game, third party tools are a hard requirement.

I wish there was a neutral option rather than just thumbs up and thumbs down. I don't feel like the game has earned a thumbs up yet it's not bad by any means, I will try really hard to give the game a fair chance and play for a while longer but I don't hold high hopes that it'll grab me for too long. It really doesn't deserve the thumbs down, but at the same, I can't say I'd recommend to my friends.

It kinda sucks to spend forever flying somewhere to sell something and it says "You need to have elite dangerous odyssey to land here". The galaxy used to feel so vast and you could go anywhere and do whatever. Now half of it is locked behind dlc. Its a downgrade and it crushes motivation.

Genuinely game is too confusing they just gave me tutorial for how to drive the ship and how to fight that's all there is not any other tutorial. I expected it to be better that no man's sky but no it is way worse. nearly my 1 hours spend in graphics integration at the start and every time you open the game, game launcher opens and ask to login. and after not learning anything at all from the tutorial i think it is not for me. i bough few things from the station and went to another station to sell the things but cant even find the station, opened the system map and tried to find the station in it and searched for information online i cant find anything. earning money is even worse i wanted to do a trade but storage is only 4 you cant only place 4 items only. they don't even teach how to approach stations either in first play spend nearly 30mins to approach the station either i keep passing by it or flying too slow. And why the fck i have to pay 8$ to be able to walk or walk out of the ship. Play no man sky, no mans sky do way better in trading and ship control and other things all their updates are always free

There are so many games that take place in space, just go play those instead.

ship

This game just doesn't respect your time. At all. It seems to be designed this way deliberately. Most of the time you stare at your screen, at forced animations for a thousands time. Or curse at the stupid frame shift travel mechanics within the system. Then, when you arrive at a station, you spawn just a few kilometers away so you can't just engage autodock right away and must waste yet another 20 seconds getting closer. Then docking takes another 30+ seconds each time. Same for undocking, where you have to waste 30 more seconds flying away from the station before being able to engage the frame shift drive. Oh yeah, want to use station services? Wait at least 5 seconds so it boots (really an f-ing network delay of a laggy server). Want to swap some equipment? Wait another 20 seconds while the ship is "being transported to the dock". Want to undock? Well, it's not thaaaat simple, here, waste another 15 seconds while ship has to be flipped 180 degrees first. Making a jump to another system? Oh, wait another forced 10sec + the asset load time, every time. Then there are stupid forced FSD cooldowns.

All this ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ is excused as "realism". And there are people who really buy this idiotic argument. Many call this game grindy. Perhaps. But even to get to the grind you first have to waste your life staring at stupid animations. Its's neither a MMO nor a single player game. It's unique as in: it takes worst of both worlds, which is an incredible feat.

On a positive note: first dozen hours seem cool, until all the glitter wears out and starts to annoy you.

This game is truly designed for people who have no lifer. Hopefully, at least those people enjoy this game. Hopefully.
Needs work in plenty of ways, but still a great experience overall if you like space.

Elite Dangerous – 70-Hour Captain's Log

Review Status: Recommended 👍

After crossing the 70-hour mark, I can confidently say two things about this game: First, the sheer scale and the quiet, realistic simulation of the universe is mesmerizing. Second, Frontier is finally listening to its player base.

When you first start, climbing that notoriously steep learning curve and successfully docking your ship for the first time gives an incredible sense of accomplishment. Having a 1:1 scale recreation of the Milky Way spread out before you, combined with a minimalist and cinematic atmosphere that truly makes you feel like a tiny speck in the cosmos, is nothing short of magnificent. Trucking cargo across the stars, deep-space mining in pristine asteroid rings, or just cruising around is the perfect way to unwind.

So, why write this review now? Because the game has finally addressed the two biggest pain points that used to hold it back:

The Engineering Overhaul (Less Grind): The soul-crushing grind and RNG mechanics that used to dictate ship upgrades have been completely revamped. Material drop rates are much better, recipes are cheaper, and the randomness is gone. You actually feel respected as a player now.

Travel Times (SEO Technology): Staring blankly at the screen for ages just to reach a distant station is largely a thing of the past, thanks to the new Super Cruise Overcharge (SEO) frame shift technology. It keeps the overwhelming sense of galactic scale intact while eliminating the tedious, empty waiting times.

When you add the on-foot exploration (Exobiology) from the Odyssey expansion and the recent gameplay updates allowing players to colonize systems and build their own space ports, the game has truly evolved into a different beast.

The Verdict: If you want a space sim where you actually feel ownership of your ship, where flight mechanics matter, and where you can just put on your favorite playlist and lose yourself in the void, Elite Dangerous is in the best shape it has ever been. Based on my 70 hours in the cockpit, this is the most definitive and deep space experience you can get.

game runs great on my pc and has good controller support, im able to use both controller and Keyboard simultaneously and it feels great, the only hang up id have about this is the lack of direction from the get go, i played on console but i still have no idea about how to start the powerplay and how to grind rank properly and it isnt really explained (if it is i've not found it yet). a more in depth tutorial would be prudent just to give you the jist of what gameplay loops are available to you. however its still great to sink a few hours into and just do what you want.

0/1, does not let me customize how ship flies.  Flight assist should be toggleable for linear momentum and angular momentum individually, and there is no way to bind Ctrl+F to a function because it's hardcoded to show FPS (despite the fact that there are numerous ways to display FPS; it should be customizable).

Wish I could recommend it, but seeing as how X4 has some nice quality of life features for flight, like being able to customize how a ship flies (in that you can use direct mode instead of mouse relative or mouse absolute), I only wish it had the capability to explore an entire galaxy.

I'd give anything to be able to experience my first Jump again. This is the only game that has convinced me to throw down for fancy peripherals like a HOTAS and one of the few that is worth gluing a VR headset to my face for hundreds of hours. Though I've enjoyed it for years, Elite Dangerous is finally shifting into an era of community-driven change, more robust multiplayer offerings, and respect for player time. Over the last two days I found profitable enough trade routes to allow me to buy and fully outfit three of my wishlist ships, and I'm having more fun with this game now than I have in the last ten years. It is an excellent time to get in here if you haven't already.

-Get EDHM UI and customize your ship's HUD colors
-Use Inara to find profitable trade routes
-Hit up /r/EliteDangerous for insanely useful new player guides
-See you in the Black

10/10.

Elite Dangerous doesn't make you feel like the hero.

It makes you feel small.

And that's exactly why I love it.

Most space games tell you that you're special. Elite Dangerous drops you into a recreation of the Milky Way with billions of star systems and quietly reminds you that nobody knows who you are, nobody cares, and if you want to make a name for yourself, you'd better earn it.

Whether you're hauling cargo across the bubble, mining asteroids, hunting pirates, exploring the black, or building a fleet of ships, every credit feels earned. There are no shortcuts to becoming a successful commander. The journey is the game.

What keeps me coming back is the atmosphere. The first time you leave a station, jump between stars, watch a gas giant fill your canopy, or realize you're thousands of light-years from civilization, it's hard not to be impressed by the sheer scale of it all.

Elite Dangerous can be frustrating. The learning curve is steep, some grinds can be lengthy, and the game rarely holds your hand. But for players willing to invest the time, it offers experiences that few other games can match.

I've hauled cargo in a Type-6, upgraded through larger ships, survived pirate encounters, explored distant systems, and spent countless hours chasing my next goal. Every commander ends up with their own story.

**Pros:**

* Incredible sense of scale
* Beautiful galaxy and ship design
* Multiple career paths
* Immersive flight model
* Unforgettable exploration

**Cons:**

* Steep learning curve
* Some progression grinds
* Not a game that explains itself well

**Overall: 9/10**

Elite Dangerous isn't about saving the galaxy.

It's about finding your place in it.

o7 Commander.

There is so much to entertain you in this game/SIM.  Value for money? Hell yes!  Started playing on console 9 years ago and made the switch to pc/steam deck just before Xmas 2025.  The community is incredible, so much effort by people developing apps (shout out to EdCopilot and srv survey) or community organised events (dw3 was superb).  This is the ultimate sandbox, flight physics are sublime (especially with flight assist off), graphics are superb despite the game being 14 years old.  Can be overwhelming for a new player but head over to the new pilot's initiative squadron for help.  Despite years in the game I have slightly dipped my toe into the 1:1 recreation of the 400billion star simulation of our galaxy.  No there isn't a story but there is so much to do, from mining to combat, exploration to trade, bounty hunt, pirate, play in solo or a private server or get ganked in open play the galaxy is insane.

I absolutely love ED. I’ve been fascinated with space ever since I was a kid, and this game completely delivers that experience for me. Whether I’m exploring new systems, trading, mining, or getting into combat, the game always feels immersive and rewarding.

If Sea of Thieves is the video game translation of "playing pirate" on the playground or at recess in school, then this game is the "space game" you may have played in a cardboard box as a child.

If you want Skyrim levels of freedom in a 1:1 scale Milky Way Galaxy with some of the best ship piloting in the industry since 1984 with the original ELITE, that you can experience with your friends - this is a game for you friend. 

I STRONGLY RECOMMEND buying a HOTAS (stick and throttle) to play this game.

If you do, do not cheap out. Shovel snow or mow lawns, ask Grandma for christmas, something to get your hands on a HOTAS in the pricepoint of about 150$, this tier gets you probably the best experience for a ton of games with piloting. I myself am a child of Star Wars Galaxies Jump To Lightspeed.

It's a grind to reach end game but you can do whatever you want either way you play it. A good way to describe the game is adaptation. There's a learning curve and grind every time you want to "take it to the next level." This game has been a challenge but I've grown to meet it. There's PVP too, you can be a bounty hunter, or a pirate terrorizing everyone. Personally I'm saving up for a fleet carrier by exploring the unexplored in the universe and coming back to sell and maps and data for billions.

loads of changes over the past year. happy to see. though the game still has some major issues in the intended game loop that were never really ironed out. this shouldnt be a deterrent, plus, frontier working with its player base to improve the experience is a huge selling point in my book. that being said. i have a note for frontier, and i hope they read this. and you can quote me on this  [IF ELITE SHIPS FELT AND FLEW LIKE STAR CITIZENS SHIPS, HAD SHIP INTERIORS, AND YOU ADDED RACING I WOULD PROBABLY SELL MY STAR CITIZEN ACCOUNT]
with that ill add one annoyance. the fact i cant have a friend provide overwatch CAS in a ship while im on the ground infiltrating a base or compound, feels like a missed opportunity. i get the choice was made for balance, but making ship weapons useless against ground troops and making ground troop weapons do insane damage to a ships shield and hull seems like the wrong choice. we want CAS options!

Not being too familiar with flight simulators, but having a passion for space, I picked up this game some months ago. The learning curve immediately struck me, but as I progressed learning the basic fundamentals it became more fun. Then I got into the grind to get credits by doing black box salvage missions and deliveries as I knew I wasn't good enough for combat yet, and that got old so I stopped playing. 
Currently I am drunk and high on cocaine writing this, so I must add that I recently picked this game up again after the Artemis II mission, and since then I haven't stopped playing. I had no idea of the third-party resources available TO the community, BY the community, which is what I respect. The ability to look up the nearest supplies, stars, ships, equipment, etc, without having to guess by jumping from system to system is amazing. The game doesn't have much hand-holding, so these resources help immensely.
To a new player first jumping in, it can be daunting. However if you love space and exploration like me, you will learn to love this game.

LOVE this game. Originally played on consoles but switched over to PC when they stopped supporting updates to consoles. The game has something for everyone. You can be space trucking one minute and fighting alien ships the next. 

There is a huge (and amazing) community behind this game. Check out Hutton Orbital for some tunes and watch some YouTube videos to see all the different third party apps you can run while you are playing. One overlay is SRV Survey (which I highly recommend for exobiology). 

I could play this game for hours just flying out and exploring. This game takes place with a 1:1 scale version of our Milky Way galaxy. Which means you can fly to the center of the galaxy and see Sagittarius A* (a supermassive black hole) which I also recommend!

Excellent game for Space Immersion. Absolutely amazing

This game is one of the best space sims out there, definitely on par with games like No Man's Sky. The universe truly feels alive, not to mention the developers use of real astronomical data to create a stunning 1-1 representation of the milky way galaxy. A lot of the negative reviews are simply based on the reviewers personal preferences, and if it's on sale, getting Odyssey is worth it, although if its not on sale I wouldn't recommend it. One thing to mention is that the game gets VERY grindy so if that's not your thing maybe reconsider. The game's realistic nature really makes you feel as if you are piloting a starship in the far future, while still keeping that jaw dropping, space opera scale that many sci-fi fans love. The starship controls are difficult at first and the game does not babysit you, but when you get the hang of them you can do some truly awesome stuff. Elite Dangerous is a must-have for any fan of space sims.

Starts game. Game start me in the tutorial.
Have to do something, so close game down again.
starts game again.
game now start in normal game world, tutorial has been skipped.
no idea what any of the controls are, but start the autopilot to leave the starting station by accident.
in space, turns around to re-dock with the station.
gets a fine for 20% of my starting money for unautorized attempt at docking.
close game again, for something else.
reenters game and now have access to the tutorials.
do tutorial and learns the basics.
pays off the fine, and then do a few data delivery missions between star systems to earn a few credits.
pick up a salvage mission, sounds easy enough.
fly to the target star system, find the item I need to salvage.
don't know how to salvage things, figures out I probably need a cargo pickup module.
fly to starbase to buy cargo pickup module.
fly back out, finds salvage item again, and turns out the cargo pickup module needs a store of robots.
fly back to starbase to buy cargo pickup module robots.
fly back out again, finds salvage item again again, and activates cargo robot.
cargo robot pick up salvage cargo, but cannot dock, because cargo door is closed.
looks up flight controls in settings, find out how to open the cargo door, and finally picks up the salvage.
delivers the salvage at the mission location.

and that was the first 5 hours.
was fun while it lasted. its fine as a space game but like main literally any other game.
